__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_0__: SaaS Acquisition Red Flags: 12 Warning Signs for Buyers | Churn Lens
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_16__: 12 specific warning signs every buyer should check before acquiring a SaaS business. Each flag is detectable from subscription CSV data — if you know what to look for.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_17__: Most SaaS acquisitions that go wrong share the same warning signs. The problem isn't that buyers can't see them — it's that they don't know where to look. Here are 12 red flags detectable from raw subscription data, ranked by severity. Any single flag warrants investigation; three or more is a strong signal to walk away or significantly reprice.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_19__: The seller reports monthly churn of 2% — but they're excluding trial cancellations, counting downgrades as "plan changes" rather than churn, or only counting customers who explicitly cancelled (missing failed-payment churn). Always recalculate churn from raw CSV data.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_1__: 12 specific red flags that signal a SaaS acquisition is overvalued or risky: hidden churn, concentration risk, declining NRR, annual cliffs, zombie accounts, and more.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_22__: If the top 3 customers represent more than 40% of MRR, the business has a single-point-of-failure risk. One whale departure can wipe out a year of growth. Calculate the
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_26__: NRR trending downward over 3+ consecutive quarters means existing customers are worth less every month. Even if headline MRR is growing (thanks to new acquisition), the revenue base is rotting from within.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_29__: If 50%+ of MRR is on annual plans and a cluster of renewals hits the same month, you're buying a revenue cliff. The low monthly churn looks great — until renewal day.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_2__: SaaS Acquisition Red Flags: 12 Warning Signs for Buyers
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_32__: 10-20% of customers haven't logged in for 90+ days but are still paying. This revenue is statistically certain to cancel within months. Subtract it from MRR before valuing the business.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_35__: If one channel (e.g., Google Ads, a single affiliate, one integration marketplace) drives 70%+ of new signups, the business is one algorithm change or partnership termination away from zero growth. Diversified acquisition is essential for durable revenue.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_37__: If the founder is the sole salesperson, handles all key customer relationships, or is the only person who understands the codebase, the business loses significant value on day one post-acquisition. Check whether customer relationships are institutionalized or personal.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_39__: If customers who signed up 6 months ago retain worse than customers who signed up 12 months ago, the product is losing product-market fit. New features, market shifts, or competitive pressure are eroding value. Always
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_3__: 12 specific red flags every SaaS buyer must check before closing. Hidden churn, concentration, zombie accounts, and more.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_42__: MRR is rising but login frequency, feature usage, or API calls are flat or declining. This means expansion revenue is coming from price increases or seat inflation, not genuine value growth. It's unsustainable and will reverse at renewal.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_43__: High Growth Fueled Entirely by New Acquisition
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_44__: If gross revenue retention is below 80% but the business is still growing, it means new customer acquisition is masking a leaking bucket. The moment acquisition slows (budget cut, channel saturation), MRR collapses. Check
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_46__: Pricing Below Market with No Path to Increase
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_47__: The SaaS charges significantly below market rate — and customers are locked into legacy pricing. Post-acquisition, price increases will trigger massive churn. The "growth opportunity" of raising prices is actually a churn bomb.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_48__: Inconsistent Billing or Data Quality Issues
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_49__: If the subscription CSV has duplicate records, inconsistent plan naming, missing signup dates, or unexplained MRR fluctuations, the data integrity is questionable. Sellers who can't provide clean data are either disorganized or hiding something. Both are reasons to discount heavily or walk away.
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_4__: SaaS Acquisition Red Flags: 12 Warning Signs for Buyers

__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_54__: These 12 red flags aren't independent — they compound. A business with hidden churn (flag 1)
__TR_saas-acquisition-red-flags_58__: declining cohorts (flag 8) is likely to lose 30-50% of its revenue within 12 months of acquisition. The interaction between flags is more dangerous than any single warning sign.
